risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to the treatment or control group in a 1 : 1 ratio and stratified on BMI category (overweight: BMI 25–29.99 versus obese: 30–39.99), age (≥40 years), and current antidepressant medication use (n = 7) ... Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition." Comment: This describes a clear random allocation method using computer-generated random numbers, with stratification, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition. After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: Although the paper does not explicitly say "allocation concealment," the assignment was generated by a statistician and only communicated to study staff after baseline assessments were completed, which indicates recruiters/enrolling staff could not foresee the next allocation before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." Comment: This was a behavioral mindfulness intervention versus waitlist control, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded; because the outcomes included self-report measures (e.g., stress, anxiety, eating behavior) and behavioral/biological outcomes that could be influenced by knowledge of assignment and expectations, lack of blinding plausibly could affect results across the study overall.

Quote: "Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments." Comment: Outcome assessment was mixed. For the primary biological/anthropometric outcomes, the assessors were explicitly blinded and the measurements (DEXA/weight/waist, blood draws, lab assays) are objective and unlikely to be influenced by allocation knowledge. Although research assistants were not blinded for questionnaires/home saliva instructions, the main outcomes reported here include objective measures, so detection bias for these outcomes is low.

Quote: "Five treatment and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis" and "Primary analyses used independent samples t-tests to test between group differences in change scores. Assuming participants lost to followup did not change over time, missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values." Comment: Attrition was modest overall (7/47 randomized; about 15%) and only slightly higher in the treatment arm than the control arm. The paper also used an intention-to-treat approach with baseline-value imputation for missing postintervention data, and there is no indication that dropout reasons were related to outcomes or that missingness was substantial enough to bias the main result.
